FAQs Content What is comprehensive genomic profiling? Comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) is a method of testing tumors that uses next-generation sequencing (NGS) to detect the four main classes of alterations known to drive cancer growth: base substitutions, insertions and deletions, copy number alterations (CNAs), and rearrangements or fusions across 50+ genes. This testing can be performed on multiple specimen types including FFPE tissue, peripheral whole blood, bone marrow aspirate. CGP helps you better predict response or resistance to targeted- and immuno- therapies for more advanced cancer patients as compared to traditional molecular testing. How is comprehensive genomic profiling different from single-gene or “hot spot” testing? Single biomarker testing identifies alterations that are confined to a single gene, potentially missing clinically relevant mutations in additional genes. Gene hotspot testing may test multiple genes at a time but is confined to commonly altered regions within those genes, resulting in potentially missing other clinically relevant classes of alterations. Comprehensive genomic profiling detects the four main classes of genomic alterations across a broader panel of genes, oftentimes hundreds. Which genes are tested by Foundation Medicine’s CGP portfolio? Foundation Medicine tests look at more than 300 cancer-related genes: FoundationOne ® CDx ( 324 DNA genes interrogated from a tissue sample) FoundationOne ® Liquid CDx ( 324 DNA genes 7 interrogated from a simple blood draw) FoundationOne ® Heme ( 406 DNA and 265 RNA genes interrogated from a variety of sample options) FoundationOne ® RNA ( 318 RNA genes interrogated from a tissue sample) Explore our gene coverage by test How do I order a test? Online Ordering : Order online by signing up for a Foundation Medicine account. EMR Integration : Order Foundation Medicine tests and receive results within your EMR. Email/Fax Ordering : To order by email, download and complete a test requisition form , along with attachments (pathology report, insurance information, and Advance Beneficiary Notice if required), and email to client.services@foundationmedicine.com. You can also place an order via fax by sending a completed test requisition form along with attachments to 617-418-2290 . Visit our Order a Test page to order specimen shipping kits for your practice. How do I obtain the results of a test? Foundation Medicine test reports can be accessed as soon as results are ready via our digital reporting portal – log in or sign up for a Foundation Medicine account to obtain access. You can also receive access to results via secure email or request to have results faxed to you by reaching out to our Client Services team at client.services@foundationmedicine.com , by phone at + 1 (888) 988-3639 or via EMR integration. Is your lab CLIA certified and CAP accredited? All Foundation Medicine comprehensive genomic profiling tests are run in CAP accredited and CLIA certified labs. View our certification documents here . What is the patient out-of-pocket cost for Foundation Medicine testing? Patient out-of-pocket costs will vary depending on your insurance. For those with out-of-pocket costs, financial assistance may be available. 8 Anyone can apply at any time to see if they qualify. Complete an online form or this PDF application . Or talk to a Client Services team member at: Phone: 888.988.3639 Email: client.services@foundationmedicine.com Important Safety Information FoundationOne CDx FoundationOne CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes as well as genomic signatures including microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB) and is a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment.  A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Some patients may require a biopsy which may pose a risk. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and important risk information, please visit www.F1CDxLabel.com . FoundationOne Liquid CDx FoundationOne Liquid CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes utilizing circulating cell-free DNA and is FDA-approved to report short variants in 311 genes, rearrangements in 8 genes and copy number alterations in 3 genes, and as a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. When considering eligibility for ROZLYTREK® based on the detection of NTRK1/2/3 and ROS1 fusions, or for TEPMETKO® based on the detection of MET SNVs and indels that lead to MET exon 14 skipping, testing using plasma specimens is only appropriate for patients for whom tumor tissue is not available for testing. Patients who are negative for companion diagnostic mutations should be reflexed to tumor tissue testing and mutation status confirmed using an FDA-approved tumor tissue test, if feasible. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and complete risk information, please visit www.F1LCDxLabel.com . FoundationOne RNA FoundationOne RNA is a laboratory developed test that was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine. FoundationOne RNA has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. FoundationOne RNA is a test for solid tumors which utilizes RNA sequencing to interrogate 318 cancer-related genes to capture gene fusions and rearrangements. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Genomic findings are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. FoundationOne Heme FoundationOne Heme is a laboratory developed test that was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine. FoundationOne Heme has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. For more information on FoundationOne Heme, please see its Technical Specifications at foundationmedicine.com/heme . References References Collapse Median, from specimen receipt to report. Medicare administered by federal government. Medicare administered by private insurers. body: Ordering and Logistics Ordering and Logistics Foundation Medicine is a partner to healthcare providers, and that means providing you with the integrations, information, and support you need to provide the best care possible to your patients. Order a Test Provider Portal Login Get Expert Support How to Order Ordering a Foundation Medicine test is simple and can be done online or through your care team. Foundation Medicine tests must be ordered by a licensed healthcare provider. 1 Choose the appropriate test Select the Foundation Medicine test that's right for your case, including: FoundationOne®CDx  + or - FoundationOne®RNA FoundationOne®Liquid CDx FoundationOne®Heme Please refer to the guides and sample requirements provided for each test. 2 Submit the order You can place your order via: your EMR system (Epic, OncoEMR, etc.) the Foundation Medicine online portal email or fax, using the downloadable Test Requisition Form Download Form 3 Prepare and send the sample Each test has specific requirements for collecting and submitting a tissue, blood, or marrow sample. When authorized, Foundation Medicine can also obtain tissue samples directly from pathology. 4 Receive and review results Turnaround times as fast as 5 days, with a median turnaround time of 7.6 days for CDx tests 1 . Access your test reports via: your EMR system (Epic, OncoEMR, etc.) the Foundation Medicine online portal fax or email Need Help? Contact Client Services Phone: 888.988.3639 | Email: client.services@foundationmedicine.com Order a Test Provider Access EMR Integration Foundation Medicine integrates into all EMRs, making your clinical workflow more efficient, and bringing all of your patients' clinical and genomic data together in one, easy-to-access interface. Provider Portal EMR Partnerships We’ve partnered with leading EMR vendors for integrated test ordering, and test results (PDF and discrete data) that show up directly in your patient charts. OncoEMR Epic Oracle/Cerner MEDITECH EMR Integration & Data Transfer Order a test without even leaving your EMR. Foundation Medicine integrates into all EMRs to enhance your clinical workflow efficiency and create easy access to patients’ clinical and genomic data all in one place. By partnering with leading EMR vendors (Epic, OncoEMR, Oracle/Cerner, MEDITECH and others), Foundation Medicine can support your health system or care network to implement seamless integration for test ordering and results (PDF and discrete data) directly into your patient chart. OncoEMR Epic Supported Test Types The Foundation Medicine portfolio is available for integration within your EMR, including: Comprehensive Portfolio: Foundation One ® CDx Foundation One ® RNA Foundation One ® LIQUID CDx Foundation One ® HEME Foundation One ® GERMLINE Foundation One ® GERMLINE MORE IHC tests: Dako PD-L1 22C3 pharmDx Dako PD-L1 28-8 pharmDx Ventana PD-L1 SP142 Ventana PD-L1 SP263 Ventana HER2 (4B5) Antibody Ventana FOLR1 (FOLR1-2.1) RxDx VENTANA ® MET (SP44) RxDx VENTANA ® MMR RxDx Foundation Medicine fully supports the transfer of genomic data in multiple formats including VCF, JSON, XML, BAM etc. for research purposes. For questions regarding our EMR integrations or data transfers, please contact our EMR Integration Specialist, or reach out to our team at Phone : +1 (888) 988-3639 | Email : Commercial.Programs@FoundationMedicine.com Online Ordering & Digital Reporting The Foundation Medicine Digital Experience Foundation Medicine's online portal can guide you and your care team through the ordering experience, from test selection to auto-filled fields. We also make it easy to upload supplemental information like insurance details, pathology reports, ABNs, and Letters of Medical Necessity (LMN). By offering customized guidance, we're committed to reducing entry errors and omissions that could delay your order. Sending in a Specimen The procedure for sending in a specimen for testing depends on the type of test. FFPE Tissue Sample If collecting the sample in-office, please follow these instructions: Download Full Instructions 1 Request a Specimen Shipping Kit to your practice using the form below, or by contacting our client services team. 2 Once the kit arrives, follow the enclosed instructions to collect and prepare the tissue sample. Only FFPE specimens will be accepted—see the instructions for further details. 3 Send the sample overnight to Foundation Medicine using the FedEx pack included in the kit. 4 Once Foundation Medicine receives the specimen, you will typically receive online or printed results of a FoundationOne CDx test within 10 days. Direct Pathologist Communication If you would like Foundation Medicine to contact the pathologist directly, please indicate this in your test order. They will receive instructions for sample procurement and shipment. Peripheral Whole Blood If collecting the sample in-office, please follow these instructions. Download Full Instructions 1 Check the expiration date on the kit exterior. Do not use expired tubes or tubes with cloudy contents or foreign matter. 2 Collect two tubes of whole blood, 8.5 mL per tube. Label each tube with the date of collection and two patient identifiers. Keep at room temperature. 3 Immediately mix each tube by gentle inversion 8 to 10 times. Ship the sample overnight on the same day of collection. 4 Foundation Medicine must receive the sample within 14 days of collection. Turnaround times as fast as 5 days, with a median turnaround time of 7.6 days for CDx tests 2 . Important Handling Requirements Use only the tubes provided in the kit. Do not freeze or refrigerate blood samples or the temperature stability gel pack. Ensure tube labels and the Test Requisition Form include matching patient identifiers. Each kit is for one patient only. Additional Ordering Options Mobile Phlebotomy Service For tests that require a liquid biopsy, Foundation Medicine offers in-home blood draw services through a mobile phlebotomy vendor at no additional cost. You can request this service while placing your order online, or by indicating it on your test requisition form. If requested, we will schedule a phlebotomist appointment directly with the patient and ship a kit to their home. When you might want to request an in-home blood draw? Patient lives far from your practice Patient is more comfortable in the privacy of their own home Patient lives near one of our patient phlebotomy centers Patient is not feeling well enough to travel No on-site phlebotomy at your practice What can you expect? Day 1 Request “Mobile Phlebotomy” when ordering the test through your EMR, online portal, or test requisition form. Day 2-4 Someone from our Client Services team will contact your patient to schedule an appointment with one of our vendors. Day 4-7 A licensed phlebotomist will perform the blood draw, and overnight the sample to Foundation Medicine’s lab. After 7 days Once the sample is received at our lab, you will typically get results within 7 days for FoundationOne Liquid CDx, or 11 days for FoundationOne Heme. Portfolio Reflex Option Select Portfolio Reflex to enable automatic reflex between FoundationOne CDx and FoundationOne Liquid CDx if the initial specimen does not meet testing criteria. Choose your preferred initial test and indicate “Portfolio Reflex” on the Test Requisition Form. If the specimen is insufficient, we notify you and proceed with the alternate test, coordinating collection as needed. When might you want to select Portfolio Reflex? Specimen may be insufficient. When tissue quantity or quality may not meet criteria. Limited tissue availability. When archival tissue may not be adequate. Avoid reordering delays. When you want automatic transition to the alternate test. Need timely results. When treatment decisions depend on faster turnaround. Streamlined workflow. When you prefer specimen coordination without placing a new order. What can you expect? Step 1 Select FoundationOne ® CDx or FoundationOne Liquid ® CDx and check “Portfolio Reflex” on the Test Requisition Form. Step 2 We proceed with the initial test. If the specimen does not meet criteria, you are notified. Step 3 If needed, Client Services coordinates collection of the alternate specimen type. Step 4 The unsuccessful test is not billed. Results are typically reported within two weeks of receipt of the new sample. Request a Specimen Shipping Kit Complete this form to request a shipping kit for your specimen.  Remember to indicate what kind of kit you need, which is determined by the test you are ordering. If you are having Foundation Medicine contact a pathologist directly, there’s no need to complete this form. You can also download the form and fill it out manually. If you’d like to order additional kits, have any questions, or are based outside of the United States, please contact client services at +1 (888) 988-3639 or client.services@foundationmedicine.com . FAQ Answers to the most common questions asked by healthcare providers about Foundation Medicine testing. Who do I contact about signing up my practice for a Foundation Medicine EMR integration? To learn more about our EMR integrations, please contact your Commercial Program Manager or reach out to our Client Services team at client.services@foundationmedicine.com or +1 (888) 988-3639 . Important Safety Information FoundationOne®CDx FoundationOne ® CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes as well as genomic signatures including microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB) and is a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment.  A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Some patients may require a biopsy which may pose a risk. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and important risk information, please visit www.F1CDxLabel.com . FoundationOne®Liquid CDx FoundationOne ® Liquid CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes utilizing circulating cell-free DNA and is FDA-approved to report short variants in 311 genes, rearrangements in 8 genes and copy number alterations in 3 genes, and as a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. When considering eligibility for ROZLYTREK ® based on the detection of NTRK1/2/3 and ROS1 fusions, or for TEPMETKO® based on the detection of MET SNVs and indels that lead to MET exon 14 skipping, testing using plasma specimens is only appropriate for patients for whom tumor tissue is not available for testing. Patients who are negative for companion diagnostic mutations should be reflexed to tumor tissue testing and mutation status confirmed using an FDA-approved tumor tissue test, if feasible. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and complete risk information, please visit www.F1LCDxLabel.com . FoundationOne®RNA FoundationOne ® RNA is a laboratory developed test that was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine. FoundationOne RNA has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. FoundationOne RNA is a test for solid tumors which utilizes RNA sequencing to interrogate 318 cancer-related genes to capture gene fusions and rearrangements. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Genomic findings are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. FoundationOne®Heme FoundationOne ® Heme is a laboratory developed test that was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine. FoundationOne Heme has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. For more information on FoundationOne Heme, please see its Technical Specifications at foundationmedicine.com/heme . FoundationOne®Germline and FoundationOne®Germline More FoundationOne ® Germline and FoundationOne ® Germline More are laboratory developed tests that were developed, and their performance characteristics determined by Fulgent Genetics. FoundationOne Germline and FoundationOne Germline More have not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Claudin 18 IHC CLDN18 IHC is a qualitative immunohistochemical assay that uses the VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) RDx Assay kit. The VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) RDx Assay, using mouse monoclonal anti-claudin 18 clone 43-14A, is intended for laboratory use in the assessment of claudin 18 (CLDN18) protein in formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) gastric adenocarcinoma including gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) tissue specimens by light microscopy. CLDN18 protein expression is determined by the percentage of viable tumor cells demonstrating moderate to strong membranous CLDN18 staining above background. This product is intended for in vitro diagnostic use. For additional information, refer to the VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) RDx Assay package insert, #740-7037. The kit for this test has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Performance characteristics were verified by Foundation Medicine, Inc., per Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA 88) requirements and in accordance with the College of American Pathologists (CAP). For prescription use only. ER IHC ER IHC is a qualitative immunohistochemical assay that uses the CONFIRM anti-Estrogen Receptor (anti-ER) (SP1) IHC Assay kit. The CONFIRM anti-Estrogen Receptor (anti-ER) (SP1) IHC Assay, using the VENTANA CONFIRM anti-ER (SP1) Rabbit Monoclonal Primary Antibody, is intended for the qualitative detection of estrogen receptor (ER) antigen in sections of formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded breast tissue on a VENTANA automated slide stainer with VENTANA detection kits and ancillary reagents. This antibody is indicated as an aid in the management, prognosis, and prediction of hormone therapy for breast carcinoma. This product is intended for in vitro diagnostic use. For additional information, refer to the CONFRIM anti-ER (SP1) Rabbit Monoclonal Antibody package inserts, # 790-4324 and # 790-4325. The kit for this test has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Performance characteristics were verified by Foundation Medicine, Inc., per Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA 88) requirements and in accordance with the College of American Pathologists (CAP). For prescription use only. HER2 (485) IHC HER2 (485) IHC is a qualitative immunohistochemical assay using rabbit monoclonal anti-HER2. VENTANA PATHWAY anti-HER-2/neu (4B5) is intended for use in the semi-quantitative detection of HER2 antigen by IHC in sections of formalin fixed, paraffin-embedded breast carcinoma and/or biliary tract cancer tissue using the ultraView Universal DAB Detection Kit on a BenchMark ULTRA instrument. In addition, the test has been analytically validated for the performance of testing gastric or gastroesophageal (GEJ) adenocarcinoma and other solid tumors, as indicated in Table 2. This product is intended for in vitro diagnostic use. Interpretation is performed in accordance with professional society guidelines. For additional information, refer to the VENTANA PATHWAY anti-HER-2/neu (4B5) Rabbit Monoclonal Primary Antibody Package Insert, #790-2991. The kit for this test has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Performance characteristics were verified by Foundation Medicine, Inc., per Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA ‘88) requirements and in accordance with the College of American Pathologists (CAP). For prescription use only. FOLR1 IHC FOLR1 IHC is a qualitative immunohistochemical assay that uses the VENTANA FOLR1 (FOLR1-2.1) RDx Assay kit. The VENTANA FOLR1 (FOLR1-2.1) RDx Assay, using mouse monoclonal anti-FOLR1 clone FOLR1-2.1, is intended for laboratory use in the assessment of folate receptor alpha (FOLR1) protein in formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer tissue specimens by light microscopy. FOLR1 protein expression is determined by the percentage of viable tumor cells demonstrating moderate and/or strong membranous FOLR1 staining. This product is intended for in vitro diagnostic use. For additional information, refer to the VENTANA FOLR1 (FOLR1-2.1) RDx Assay package insert, #740-5065. The kit for this test has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Performance characteristics were verified by Foundation Medicine, Inc., per Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA 88) requirements and in accordance with the College of American Pathologists (CAP). For prescription use only. MET IHC MET IHC is a qualitative immunohistochemical assay that uses the VENTANA MET (SP44) RxDx Assay kit. The VENTANA MET (SP44) RxDx Assay, using rabbit monoclonal anti-MET, clone SP44, is intended for laboratory use in the assessment of MET protein in formalin-fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) tissue by light microscopy. MET protein expression in non-squamous NSCLC is determined by the percentage of viable tumor cells with membranous and/or cytoplasmic staining at 3+ intensity. This product is intended for in vitro diagnostic use. For additional information, refer to the VENTANA MET (SP44) RxDx Assay package insert, #740-7064. The kit for this test has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Performance characteristics were verified by Foundation Medicine, Inc., per Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA 88) requirements and in accordance with the College of American Pathologists (CAP). For prescription use only MMR IHC VENTANA MMR RxDx Panel is a qualitative immunohistochemical test that uses the VENTANA MMR RxDx kit. The VENTANA MMR RxDx panel, using the VENTANA anti-MLH1 (M1) Mouse Monoclonal Primary antibody, VENTANA anti-PMS2 (A16-4) Mouse Monoclonal Primary Antibody, VENTANA anti-MSH2 (G219-1129) Mouse Monoclonal Primary Antibody, and VENTANA anti-MSH6 (SP93) Rabbit Monoclonal Primary Antibody kits, is intended for laboratory use in the assessment of mismatch repair (MMR) proteins (MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, and MSH6) in formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue specimens by light microscopy. VENTANA MMR RxDx Panel is indicated as an aid in identifying patients eligible for treatment with the therapies listed in Table 1 for the indication and MMR status in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. The Dako PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx Assay, using mouse monoclonal anti-PD-L1 clone 22C3, is intended for use in the assessment of PD-L1 protein in formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC), cervical cancer, head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC), triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), and gastric or gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) adenocarcinoma tissues using the EnVision FLEX visualization system on Autostainer Link 48. PD-L1 protein expression in NSCLC is determined by using Tumor Proportion Score (TPS), which is the percentage of viable tumor cells showing partial or complete membrane staining at any intensity. This product is intended for in vitro diagnostic use. For additional information, refer to the PD-L1 IHC 22C3 pharmDx package insert. Performance characteristics were verified by Foundation Medicine, Inc., per Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA ’88) requirements and in accordance with the College of American Pathologists (CAP). For prescription use only. PD-L1 IHC 28-8 PD-L1 28-8 IHC is a qualitative immunohistochemical assay that uses the Dako PD-L1 IHC 28-8 pharmDx™ Assay kit. The Dako PD-L1 IHC 28-8 pharmDx Assay, using rabbit monoclonal anti-PD-L1 clone 28-8, is intended for use in the assessment of PD-L1 protein in formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (SCCHN), and urothelial carcinoma (UC) tissues using EnVision FLEX visualization system on Autostainer Link 48. PD-L1 protein expression is defined as the percentage of evaluable tumor cells exhibiting partial or complete membrane staining at any intensity. This product is intended for in vitro diagnostic use. For additional information, refer to the PD-L1 IHC 28-8 pharmDx package insert. The kit for this test has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Performance characteristics were verified by Foundation Medicine, Inc., per Clinical Laboratory. PD-L1 SP142 IHC PD-L1 SP142 IHC is a qualitative immunohistochemical assay that uses the VENTANA PD-L1 (SP142) Assay kit. The VENTANA PD-L1 (SP142) Assay, using rabbit monoclonal anti-PD-L1 clone SP142, is intended for use in the assessment of the PD-L1 protein in formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) tissue stained with OptiView DAB IHC Detection Kit and OptiView Amplification Kit on a VENTANA BenchMark ULTRA instrument. Determination of PD-L1 status is indication-specific, and evaluation is based on either the proportion of tumor area occupied by PD-L1 expressing tumor-infiltrating immune cells (% IC) of any intensity or the percentage of PD-L1 expressing tumor cells (% TC) of any intensity. title: FoundationOne Germline | Foundation Medicine
body: Reveal Hereditary Risk to Guide Care Order a Test Latest Research Overview Image FOUNDATION ONE GERMLINE Foundation Medicine offers two different germline tests. When used in conjunction with one of our CGP assays, they can provide a more comprehensive molecular profile to help guide clinical management. Germline tests can identify genetic variants linked to hereditary cancers, and help you understand risks for your patients and their families. Supporting Documents How to Order Test Details Methodology Next-generation sequencing Sample Type Blood sample or saliva swab Turnaround Time 2-3 weeks Genes Analyzed Up to 154 genes Compare Our Tests Both our germline assays are laboratory-developed tests powered by our reference laboratory, Fulgent Genetics . Each test requires a separate specimen, even if ordered in conjunction with other Foundation Medicine tests. Supporting Documents To order: Visit our Online Portal or order through your EMR. Germline Testing can be ordered as a standalone assay or with one of Foundation Medicine's CGP assays. Order a Test Portal Access Resources & Publications Explore our database of Foundation Medicine resources and publications. Explore All Resources PDF file Test Requisition Form (TRF) Download PDF file Germline Testing One Pager Download PDF file FoundationOne Germline About the Tests Download PDF file FoundationOne Germline Specimen Instructions Download FAQ Answers to the most common questions asked by healthcare providers about Foundation Medicine testing. FAQs Content Which genes are included in your germline test offerings? The complete gene lists for both FoundationOne®Germline and FoundationOne ® Germline More can be found on page 2 of our Germline Testing Overview . What are the specimen requirements for testing? Specimen collection kits for germline testing—either blood draw or saliva sample—can be requested by contacting Foundation Medicine at 888-988-3639 or client.services@foundationmedicine.com . How is billing handled? Billing is handled by our reference laboratory, Fulgent Genetics. Please direct any billing inquiries regarding germline testing to 1-888-385-4368 or billing@fulgentgenetics.com . Is financial assistance available? Financial assistance is available for qualifying patients based on financial need. Fulgent’s application for their financial assistance program can be found here . What insurance is accepted for germline testing? Medicare, Medicaid, and all commercial insurance plans are accepted for both FoundationOne Germline and FoundationOne Germline More for qualifying patients. Actual coverage for the testing will vary depending on the patient’s insurance plan and coverage policies. For additional information about insurance and billing, please contact Fulgent Genetics at 1-888-385-4368 or via email billing@fulgentgenetics.com . Is genetic counseling available for patients? Post-test genetic counseling is available at no-cost to qualifying patients. Qualifying patients include all individuals who are identified to carry a diagnostic pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant through germline testing. In order to access no-cost genetic counseling, the ordering provider must submit the genetic counseling referral form to FMISupport@fulgentgenetics.com to initiate the process. Once the form is submitted, a company called DNAvisit will reach out directly to the patient to coordinate a genetic counseling session. The ordering (referring) provider will be sent a follow-up consult note once the genetic counseling session has been completed. Who should I contact if I have a question about my germline test results? In most instances, questions directly related to your germline test results can be answered most quickly by contacting our reference laboratory, Fulgent Genetics. They can be reached by calling 877-204-4319 or emailing FMISupport@fulgentgenetics.com . Make sure to identify yourself as a Foundation Medicine customer. Important Safety Information FoundationOne Germline and FoundationOne Germline More FoundationOne Germline and FoundationOne Germline More are laboratory developed tests that were developed, and their performance characteristics determined by Fulgent Genetics. FoundationOne Germline and FoundationOne Germline More have not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.

title: Preparing for Conversations | Foundation Medicine
body: Preparing for Conversations Support for Every Step and Every Conversation Financial Support Patient Resources Financial Support Conversation Guides for Every Part of Your Cancer Experience 1. Before Testing 1. Before Testing 2. Discussing Your Results 3. Support from Caregivers and Family 4. Planning Your Next Steps Ask Yourself What’s the most important thing I want to learn from this test? That could be: Understanding potential treatment options Finding a treatment with the fewest side effects Determining a personalized treatment plan Something else If your test results indicated you were a potential candidate for a clinical trial of a new treatment, would you consider that? Ask Your Care Team Has my cancer already been tested for biomarkers? What could a biomarker test tell us about my cancer? How will the test results help us plan my treatment? What happens next? Ask Yourself Which parts of the results feel clear, and which feel confusing? Do I want a high level summary or a detailed explanation? What decisions am I hoping these results will help with? Ask Your Care Team What findings are most relevant to my situation today? What do these results mean for my treatment options? Are there limitations or uncertainties I should understand? Do these results suggest options now, or possibilities to revisit later? Ask Yourself How involved do I want my caregiver or family member to be? Would it help to have someone take notes or help track questions? Are there parts of this process I do not want to manage alone? Ask Your Care Team How can caregivers best support appointments and follow up? What information can be shared, and with whom? Are there resources available for caregivers as well as patients? Who can we contact if questions come up between visits? Ask Yourself Do I feel ready to make a decision, or do I need more time? Would I like to explore a second opinion or additional testing? What concerns me most about the next phase of care? Ask Your Care Team What decisions need to be made now versus later? What options are available at this stage? How will we monitor progress or reassess if things change? Are clinical trials something we should consider now or in the future? Glossary of Key Terms A Alterations Changes in the DNA of a gene that may lead to cancer, sometimes also called “mutations” Antibody Drug Conjugates (ADCs) A type of targeted cancer treatment that can deliver cancer drugs to specific cancer cells without harming nearby healthy cells B Base Substitution A type of mutation where a base pair of the DNA is swapped for another Biomarkers A measurable marker that indicates something about a person’s biology that can influence what is causing their cancer and potentially inform treatment decisions Biomarker Testing A type of diagnostic test that looks for genes, proteins and other tumor markers to provide information about a person’s cancer, also referred to as “genomic testing” Biopsy A medical procedure that removes cells or tissue from the body for testing C Cells The individual building blocks that make up your body Circulating Tumor DNA (ctDNA) Small DNA fragments from a tumor that can be found circulating in the blood Clinical Trial A type of research study that tests how well new medical approaches, including new drugs, work in real-life Companion Diagnostic (CDx) An example of a high-quality test, which undergoes extensive testing and review by an independent agency, that provides information for the safe and effective use of a corresponding treatment Comprehensive Genomic Profiling (CGP) A type of cancer test that leverages next-generation sequencing to simultaneously analyzes 50+ cancer-related genes to detect the four main types of genomic alterations that drive tumor growth: Base substitutions (point mutations) Insertions and deletions (indels) Copy number alterations (CNAs) Rearrangements or fusions Copy Number Alterations A type of genomic alteration that develops as a result of changes to DNA structure and leads to gaining or losing large swaths of DNA, impacting the number of copies of a gene present ctDNA Tumor Fraction An estimation of the percentage of DNA found in a blood sample that is circulating tumor DNA D Diagnosis The identification of the cause of an illness; in cancer care, it involves information on tumor type and stage of cancer DNA A molecule that contains the genetic information, or a “blueprint,” that provides instructions for the operation of each individual cell. DNA is made up of four bases: adenine (A), cytosine (C), guanine (G), and thymine (T) E Exome The section of the genome which tells the body how to make proteins F Fusions A type of genomic alteration that forms when parts from two independent genes reposition and fuse together, which may lead to cancer. Sometimes also referred to as “rearrangements” G Gene Individual segments of DNA that make up a small portion of the genome Genome The complete set of genetic material that is made up of unique DNA sequences Genomic Testing A type of diagnostic test that looks for genes, proteins and other tumor markers to provide information about a person’s cancer, also referred to as a “biomarker testing” Genetic Testing A type of test that analyzes DNA to look at inherited mutations in all cells of the body that can be passed on from your parents, also referred to as “germline testing” Germline Testing A type of test that analyzes DNA to look at inherited mutations in all cells of the body that can be passed on from your parents via germ cells (aka egg and sperm cells), also referred to as “genetic testing” I Immunotherapy A type of medicine that uses a person’s own immune system to fight cancer Insertions and Deletions A type of genomic alteration where sections of DNA may be inserted or deleted in a gene, commonly referred to as “indels” L Liquid Biopsy A minimally invasive procedure to collect a sample such as blood, urine or another bodily fluids that can be analyzed for diagnosis, biomarker testing or treatment response monitoring M Molecular Residual Disease (MRD) Sometimes also called minimal or measurable residual disease, it refers to a very small number of cancer cells that remain in the body during or after treatment N Next-Generation Sequencing A sequencing method that leverages technology to rapidly read large portions of DNA or RNA to provide insights on genes R RNA A molecular similar to DNA that can play various roles in the creation of proteins and the regulation of gene expression S Solid Tumors An abnormal mass of tissue that results from the uncontrolled growth of cells. Solid tumors may be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer). Examples of solid tumor cancers include breast, prostate, lung and colorectal T Targeted Treatment A type of medicine designed to target the specific changes in cancer cells that allow them to survive and spread Tissue Groups of cells that make up organs and body parts Tissue Biopsy A medical procedure to obtain a tissue sample that can be analyzed for diagnosis or testing, such as biomarker testing Tumor A mass of tissue in the body caused by abnormal growth of cells; they can be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer) U U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) The U.S. governmental agency responsible for the review and approval of drugs and diagnostic tests to determine the safety and effectiveness for the intended use in patients W Whole Genome Sequencing A type of sequencing test that looks at the full genome, or the complete genetic makeup of DNA sequences, including both the coding sections (aka the genes) and non-coding sections, to help identify alterations that may contribute to disease Explore More Topics How Testing Works Learn how biomarker testing is performed, what it involves and what to expect step by step. Learn More Financial Support Find information about insurance coverage and financial assistance options available to patients. Learn More Resources for Patients Access guides, FAQs and educational materials that support you in testing and results. View All

title: FoundationOne Heme | Foundation Medicine
body: Deep Genomic Insights for Blood Cancers Order a Test Latest Research Download a Sample Report Overview Image FoundationOne®HEME FoundationOne ® Heme 9 , a laboratory developed test 1 , is a comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) test designed specifically for blood cancers like leukemia and lymphoma. It uses both DNA and RNA sequencing, making it especially useful for detecting genomic fusions and other kinds of genomic alterations, such as substitutions, indels, and copy number variations (CNV). Supporting Documents How to Order Test Details Methodology Next-generation sequencing Sample Type Blood sample, Tissue biopsy,  Bone marrow Turnaround Time 11 days 2 Genes Analyzed 400+ DNA & 250+ RNA Key Features Explore the ways FoundationOne ® Heme supports you and your patients. 1 Combined DNA and RNA Sequencing Using a combined approach, FoundationOne®Heme can analyze 400+ genes with DNA and 250+ genes with RNA sequencing. This allows it to detect all four main classes of genomic alterations, including sensitive identification of translocations and fusions. 2 The insights doctors need to guide treatment FoundationOne Heme’s broad range of genes includes those recommended by professional guidelines for leukemia and MDS3-5 treatment. 6 That means doctors and caregivers get the information they need to make better treatment decisions for their patients. All reports also include Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) reporting, which supports clinical interpretation, diagnosis, and prognosis for patients with blood cancers. 3 Works with blood, tissue or bone marrow FoundationOne Heme is validated for use with a wide variety of sample types. Resources & Publications Explore our database of Foundation Medicine resources and publications. FAQs Content What is comprehensive genomic profiling? Comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) is a method of testing tumors that uses next-generation sequencing (NGS) to detect the four main classes of alterations known to drive cancer growth: base substitutions, insertions and deletions, copy number alterations (CNAs), and rearrangements or fusions across 50+ genes. This testing can be performed on multiple specimen types including FFPE tissue, peripheral whole blood, and bone marrow aspirate. CGP helps you better predict response or resistance to targeted- and immuno- therapies for more advanced cancer patients as compared to traditional molecular testing. How is comprehensive genomic profiling different from single-gene or “hot spot” testing? Single biomarker testing identifies alterations that are confined to a single gene, potentially missing clinically relevant mutations in additional genes. Gene hotspot testing may test multiple genes at a time but is confined to commonly altered regions within those genes, resulting in potentially missing other clinically relevant classes of alterations. Comprehensive genomic profiling detects the four main classes of genomic alterations across a broader panel of genes, oftentimes hundreds. Which genes are tested by Foundation Medicines CGP portfolio? Foundation Medicine tests look at more than 300 cancer-related genes: FoundationOne ® CDx ( 324 DNA genes interrogated from a tissue sample) FoundationOne ® Liquid CDx ( 324 DNA genes 7 interrogated from a simple blood draw) FoundationOne ® Heme ( 406 DNA and 265 RNA genes interrogated from a variety of sample options) FoundationOne ® RNA ( 318 RNA genes interrogated from a tissue sample) Explore our gene coverage by test How do I order a test? Online Ordering : Order online by signing up for a Foundation Medicine account. EMR Integration : Order Foundation Medicine tests and receive results within your EMR. Email/Fax Ordering : To order by email, download and complete a test requisition form , along with attachments (pathology report, insurance information, and Advance Beneficiary Notice if required), and email to client.services@foundationmedicine.com . You can also place an order via fax by sending a completed test requisition form along with attachments to 617-418-2290 . Visit our Order a Test page to order specimen shipping kits for your practice. How do I obtain the results of a test? Foundation Medicine test reports can be accessed as soon as results are ready via our digital reporting portal – log in or sign up for a Foundation Medicine account to obtain access. You can also receive access to results via secure email or request to have results faxed to you by reaching out to our Client Services team at client.services@foundationmedicine.com , by phone at +1 (888) 988-3639 or via EMR integration. Is your lab CLIA certified and CAP accredited? All Foundation Medicine comprehensive genomic profiling tests are run in CAP accredited and CLIA certified labs. View our certification documents here . What is the patient out-of-pocket cost for Foundation Medicine testing? Patient out-of-pocket costs will vary depending on your insurance. For those with out-of-pocket costs, financial assistance may be available.8 Anyone can apply at any time to see if they qualify. Complete an online form or this PDF application . Or talk to a Client Services team member at: Phone: 888.988.3639 Email: client.services@foundationmedicine.com Important Safety Information FoundationOne Heme FoundationOne Heme is a laboratory developed test that was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine. FoundationOne Heme has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. For more information on FoundationOne Heme, please see its Technical Specifications at foundationmedicine.com/heme . FoundationOne CDx FoundationOne CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes as well as genomic signatures including microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB) and is a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Some patients may require a biopsy which may pose a risk. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and important risk information, please visit www.F1CDxLabel.com . FoundationOne Liquid CDx FoundationOne Liquid CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes utilizing circulating cell-free DNA and is FDA-approved to report short variants in 311 genes, rearrangements in 8 genes and copy number alterations in 3 genes, and as a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. When considering eligibility for ROZLYTREK ® based on the detection of NTRK1/2/3 and ROS1 fusions, or for TEPMETKO® based on the detection of MET SNVs and indels that lead to MET exon 14 skipping, testing using plasma specimens is only appropriate for patients for whom tumor tissue is not available for testing. Patients who are negative for companion diagnostic mutations should be reflexed to tumor tissue testing and mutation status confirmed using an FDA-approved tumor tissue test, if feasible. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and complete risk information, please visit www.F1LCDxLabel.com . FoundationOne RNA FoundationOne RNA is a laboratory developed test that was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Foundation Medicine. FoundationOne RNA has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. FoundationOne RNA is a test for solid tumors which utilizes RNA sequencing to interrogate 318 cancer-related genes to capture gene fusions and rearrangements. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Genomic findings are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Professional Services Summary Professional Services Summary FDA-Approved Claims Page Professional Services Details Professional Services Summary Located at the beginning of the report, the professional services summary includes information on all reported biomarker and genomic findings. This section is not reviewed or approved by the FDA. 1. Report Highlights This feature distills important genomic insights in one easy-to-find place, helping you focus on the key actionable results to inform your patient’s treatment plan. Such key findings may include targeted therapies with potential resistance, germline implications, non-targeted therapy implications and more depending on each patient case. 2. Therapy and Clinical Trial Implications Therapies for each associated genomic finding are listed in the therapy table. On the left are therapies within your patient’s tumor type, and on the right are those with proven clinical benefit in other tumor types. Therapy resistance based on your patient’s genomic profile will also be indicated. If there are matched clinical trials, the number of trials and the corresponding report page are listed for each biomarker or genomic finding. 3. Incidental Findings Banners Identifies potential germline or clonal hematopoiesis alterations that may
warrant follow-up testing. The appearance of the germline banner indicates that
an alteration has been previously reported in literature or genomic databases as
a germline alteration, not that it is a germline alteration in the patient’s sample. 4. Genomic Findings with no Reportable Therapeutic or Clinical Trial Options Alterations detected that currently have no associated targeted therapies or relevant clinical trial options; provided for completeness and may become actionable as new data emerge. FDA-Approved Claims Page 1. Companion Diagnostics (CDx) findings This section lists any FDA-approved companion diagnostic (CDx) claims associated with your patient’s genomic findings. Professional Services Details The final section details the remaining professional services and includes three components: 1. Biomarker and genomic findings Includes additional detail about your patient’s findings, as well as the context of those findings in the patient’s tumor type. 2. Therapeutic options Clinical evidence associated with therapeutic sensitivity or resistance for identified genomic alterations or biomarkers in the context of the patient’s tumor type are discussed in this section. 3. Clinical trial information Detailed information about the clinical trials your patient has been matched to, ranked for the patient based on location and trial phase. Still Have Questions? The test analyzes 324 genes as well as genomic signatures including microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB) and is a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment.  A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Some patients may require a biopsy which may pose a risk. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and important risk information, please visit www.F1CDxLabel.com . FoundationOne®Liquid CDx FoundationOne ® Liquid CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes utilizing circulating cell-free DNA and is FDA-approved to report short variants in 311 genes, rearrangements in 8 genes and copy number alterations in 3 genes, and as a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. FAQs Content What is comprehensive genomic profiling? Comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) is a method of testing tumors that uses next-generation sequencing (NGS) to detect the four main classes of alterations known to drive cancer growth: base substitutions, insertions and deletions, copy number alterations (CNAs), and rearrangements or fusions across 50+ genes. This testing can be performed on multiple specimen types including FFPE tissue, peripheral whole blood, bone marrow aspirate. CGP helps you better predict response or resistance to targeted- and immuno- therapies for more advanced cancer patients as compared to traditional molecular testing. How is comprehensive genomic profiling different from single-gene or “hot spot” testing? Single biomarker testing identifies alterations that are confined to a single gene, potentially missing clinically relevant mutations in additional genes. Gene hotspot testing may test multiple genes at a time but is confined to commonly altered regions within those genes, resulting in potentially missing other clinically relevant classes of alterations. Comprehensive genomic profiling detects the four main classes of genomic alterations across a broader panel of genes, oftentimes hundreds. What is FoundationOne RNA? FoundationOne RNA is a laboratory-developed test (LDT) for all solid tumors. It uses hybrid capture-based next generation sequencing (NGS) to detect cancer-related gene fusions and rearrangements from RNA across 318 genes. FoundationOne RNA testing can be added to any FoundationOne CDx order to provide additional insights and confidence in fusion detection beyond the proven DNA-based fusion detection provided by FoundationOne CDx. How do I order a FoundationOne RNA test? FoundationOne RNA is only available as an add-on to FoundationOne CDx , and must be added at the time a FoundationOne CDx order is placed. Orders can be placed through the Foundation Medicine online portal , or through an EMR integration, or by submitting a paper Test Requisition Forms (TRF) . How do I submit a specimen for FoundationOne RNA? Since FoundationOne RNA is only performed as an add-on, no additional sample or kit is needed beyond what is required for FoundationOne CDx testing. RNA is extracted through a coextraction method, using the same sample provided in the FoundationOne ® CDx kit. Is your lab CLIA certified and CAP accredited? All Foundation Medicine comprehensive genomic profiling tests are run in CAP Accredited and CLIA Certified labs. View our certification documents here . body: Financial Support Foundation Medicine is Committed to Helping Patients Access Testing At Foundation Medicine, we believe all patients should have access to high-quality testing. That is why we provide patients with financial resources and support throughout the testing process as part of our FoundationAccess ® program. Payment plans are available for patients. Financial assistance may also be available for qualifying patients. 1 Foundation Medicine will make every attempt to obtain insurance coverage. For every test ordered, we complete a benefits investigation, including helping obtain prior authorizations when required, billing your health plan for the test, and appealing denials with your consent. 86% of patients have $0 financial responsibility for testing.² Patient Financial Assistance: Through our FoundationAccess ® Program, Foundation Medicine offers a needs-based financial assistance program for qualifying patients. If there are any out-of-pocket costs for testing, patients who qualify for financial assistance will pay no more than $100 out-of-pocket for tests performed by Foundation Medicine in their lifetime regardless of the number of tests ordered. Anyone can apply at any time to see if they qualify for financial assistance. Apply Online Download a Form Support for Patients Enrolled in Medicare: Original Medicare (fee-for-service) and Medicare Advantage plans cover one or more of Foundation Medicine's tests for beneficiaries who meet coverage criteria. 3,4 While a majority of Medicare beneficiaries will not incur any out-of-pocket costs for Foundation Medicine testing, some beneficiaries may need assistance depending on program coverage. For beneficiaries with out-of-pocket costs, financial assistance is available for those who qualify. Support for Patients Enrolled in Medicaid and Children’s Health Insurance Program (CHIP): Fee-for-service Medicaid and fee-for-service CHIP beneficiaries automatically qualify for financial assistance and do not need to complete an application. Beneficiaries enrolled in a Medicaid managed care plan will need to apply for financial assistance. body: Supplier Information Foundation Medicine is committed to patients, and on this page, suppliers can learn how to realize business ambitions with us. Foundation Medicine recognizes that suppliers are a vital link to our success. Working together, we can achieve a value chain relationship that will provide customer satisfaction, sustainable competitive advantage and long-term mutual benefit. Please read on for information about our supplier expectations, requirements and procedures to conduct business with Foundation Medicine. What We Look For In Suppliers Quality – Foundation Medicine sets high standards of performance for itself and the products and services we produce. We expect the same from our suppliers across areas such as quality, responsiveness and service commitments. We appreciate any examples you can provide of excellence in your business, including awards and certifications. Customer Focus - We aspire to partner with companies who will extend the highest quality services goals and objectives to their customers. Cost Effectiveness - We value suppliers pro-actively contributing to innovative and collaborative efforts to achieve lower costs. Innovative Business Solutions - We seek out partners who are willing to work toward continuous improvement and more efficient products and services. Technology - We look for companies that optimize the use of technology to maintain a competitive edge and can add to their responsiveness. What Suppliers Can Expect From Us Information - We will provide you with as much information as possible on how our purchasing process works, as well as the specific requirements of a project. Fair treatment - Our processes are structured to ensure equal and fair treatment to your company and encourage full participation in the purchasing and contracting process. Feedback - We will provide routine feedback regarding performance and other business issues. Supplier Program At Foundation Medicine, we strategically strive to utilize an array of small and large suppliers to drive a competitive advantage. Through the use of these suppliers, Foundation Medicine can align and expand on our goals to drive innovation and collaboration by building a robust network to strategically source goods and services. Visit our Supplier Program page to learn more . Performance Guidelines At Foundation Medicine, our procurement team develops relevant key performance indicators (KPIs) to assess supplier performance. Foundation Medicine highly values the relationships we have with our many supply partners. Our ultimate success, and that of our customers and suppliers is directly related to creating an optimized supply-chain relationship with a timely and high-quality flow of goods. As part of our ongoing commitment to excellence in all aspects of our business, we regularly measure, review and improve supplier performance. Our feedback will be shared through quarterly business reviews, or as otherwise deemed necessary by procurement. Shipping & Receiving Foundation Medicine's policy is to accept only goods and services that have authorization (Purchase Order) before starting services and/or shipment and receipt of goods. The Purchase Order number should always be referenced on packing slips and all invoices. If you do not have authorization from a Foundation Medicine Procurement representative, we may choose (i) not to accept and return the goods, freight paid by you or (ii) not to accept services and reject any invoices. Please refer to our PO Terms and Conditions , and Incoterms for additional information. body: Resources Knowledge Center A precision oncology resource featuring curated cancer genomic-focused educational content with medical professionals. Stay up-to-date on this ever-expanding field. View Resources Latest Content More Latest Content Blog ctDNA Tumor Fraction Matters When Interpreting Liquid Biopsy Test Results While oncologists increasingly use liquid biopsies for genomic testing, many still have concerns that a negative result from liquid biopsy may not be meaningful enough to direct clinical decision making. This concern can be mitigated by Foundation Medicine’s new circulating tumor DNA tumor fraction which improves confidence in the meaning of a negative result from liquid biopsies. Read Article Prostate Cancer The Growing Importance of Comprehensive Genomic Profiling in Prostate Cancer Read Article Healthcare Provider Education Decoding the science of precision oncology by medical experts for medical professionals, the Knowledge Center is a uniquely informative resource dedicated to helping you identify actionable alterations and therapy options for your patients. Podcasts, articles, or publication summary videos—choose your path to explore the science underlying genomic testing. More Provider Education Decoded – Episode 1 ctDNA Tumor Fraction Matters When Interpreting Liquid Biopsy Test Results Lincoln Pasquina, PhD, Director of Clinical Development at Foundation Medicine, explains how physicians can leverage Foundation Medicine’s ctDNA tumor fraction to improve clinical care. ctDNA tumor fraction is a measure of how much ctDNA is measured in an individual liquid biopsy. Listen on Spotify Listen on Apple Podcasts Listen on iHeart Listen on Amazon Measurement of ctDNA tumor fraction identifies informative negative liquid biopsy results and informs value of tissue confirmation See the highlights of this peer-reviewed article from Clinical Cancer Research in a brief animated publication summary. Watch the video to learn how Foundation Medicine's ctDNA tumor fraction can distinguish whether tumor-derived DNA is present in a liquid biopsy sample. Using the ctDNA tumor fraction threshold of 1%+, providers can have increased confidence in negative results on a liquid biopsy. View Case Study Peer to Peer Education Pioneers in Practice is an educational webinar series that reviews the importance of Comprehensive Genomic Profiling (CGP) for patients with Advanced Breast Cancer, Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer, and Prostate Cancer. More Peer to Peer Education NSCLC A Brief Review of DNA and RNA in Advanced NSCLC with Dr. Todd M. Bauer In this brief 5-minute video, Dr. Todd M. Bauer reviews the history of next-generation sequencing for targeted therapies in advanced non-small cell lung cancer and discusses the differences between DNA and RNA-based sequencing technologies for these patients. View Recording Review of Foundation Medicine's Biomarker, ctDNA Tumor Fraction & Implications in Clinical Practice In this brief 4-minute video, Dr. Hatim Husain reviews the importance of utilizing a liquid biopsy test that reports ctDNA tumor fraction with a validated cut-off to guide clinical decision making, and how it has made an impact in his practice and with his patients. View Recording Biopharma Education Discover insights that drive innovation. Access educational resources designed to provide the latest advancements in therapy development, clinical trials, and comprehensive genomic profiling. Explore expert perspectives, in-depth guides, and actionable solutions to accelerate breakthroughs. More Biopharma Knowledge Presented at ASCO 2024 Genomic classification of clinically advanced solid tumors based on MTAP genomic loss Key Findings The genomic landscape of MTAP complete and partial loss across more than 540,000 clinical samples shows a frequency pan-cancer of 9.3% Highest frequencies in glioblastoma (43%), bladder cancer (25%), pancreatic cancer (22%), and non-small cell lung cancer (13%) MTAP loss is a frequent genomic alteration of emerging clinical importance as the clinical trials using PRMT5 and MAT2A inhibitors progress Download Poster Presented at AMP 2024 Analytical validation of a homologous recombination deficiency signature (HRDsig) in pan-tumor tissue samples Key Findings HRDsig is a pan-tumor biomarker on FoundationOne®CDx tissue-based test that does not rely on gene alterations and instead uses a DNA scar-based approach to calculate a score based on copy number features to enable detection of both genomic and non-genomic mechanisms of HRD Analytical validation demonstrated high concordance compared to an independent HRD biomarker, low false-positive rate, and high reproducibility across disease areas Download Poster Important Safety Information FoundationOne CDx FoundationOne CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes as well as genomic signatures including microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB) and is a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment.  A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. Some patients may require a biopsy which may pose a risk. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and important risk information, please visit www.F1CDxLabel.com . FoundationOne®Liquid CDx FoundationOne Liquid CDx is for prescription use only and is an FDA-approved qualitative next-generation sequencing based in vitro diagnostic test for advanced cancer patients with solid tumors. The test analyzes 324 genes utilizing circulating cell-free DNA and is FDA-approved to report short variants in 311 genes, rearrangements in 8 genes and copy number alterations in 3 genes, and as a companion diagnostic to identify patients who may benefit from treatment with specific therapies in accordance with the approved therapeutic product labeling. Additional genomic findings may be reported and are not prescriptive or conclusive for labeled use of any specific therapeutic product. Use of the test does not guarantee a patient will be matched to a treatment. A negative result does not rule out the presence of an alteration. When considering eligibility for ROZLYTREK® based on the detection of NTRK1/2/3 and ROS1 fusions, or for TEPMETKO® based on the detection of MET SNVs and indels that lead to MET exon 14 skipping, testing using plasma specimens is only appropriate for patients for whom tumor tissue is not available for testing. Patients who are negative for companion diagnostic mutations should be reflexed to tumor tissue testing and mutation status confirmed using an FDA-approved tumor tissue test, if feasible. For the complete label, including companion diagnostic indications and complete risk information, please visit www.F1LCDxLabel.com .

title: Roche receives CE Mark for blood test to identify tuberculosis infection
body: Roche receives CE Mark for blood test to identify tuberculosis infection worldwide Roche receives CE Mark for blood test to identify tuberculosis infection 2026-07-09 The Elecsys IGRA TB test expands global access to tuberculosis infection testing, advancing progress toward the World Health Organization's elimination targets. Performed on Roche’s industry-leading and widely available cobas immunoassay systems, the test enables laboratories to meet growing tuberculosis infection testing needs by reducing reliance on manual workflows through automation and digital solutions. The new solution provides rapid, high-throughput, and cost-effective tuberculosis testing, delivers reliable results in under 24 hours and cuts standard processing times in half to just 19 minutes per patient. Basel, 9 July 2026 - Roche (SIX: RO, ROP; OTCQX: RHHBY) announced today that its Elecsys IGRA TB test has received the CE Mark, introducing a new blood testing solution for identifying tuberculosis infection (TBI, also known as latent TB) in routine laboratory settings. The new solution enables fast, simple, and efficient diagnosis, and can provide results in under 24 hours. The Interferon Gamma Release Assay (IGRA) itself takes just 19 minutes per patient to process, which is approximately half the time of currently used methods. Laboratories now have an option for IGRA testing, with faster turnaround and scalable throughput on Roche’s systems. “Addressing latent tuberculosis is critical to reducing the global health burden of this devastating disease, and that begins with better, more accessible testing,” said Matt Sause, CEO of Roche Diagnostics. “The Elecsys IGRA TB test brings an automated solution for latent TB to Roche’s industry-leading immunoassay platforms, which will enable screening programs and drive significant progress toward achieving global TB elimination targets.” Tuberculosis remains one of the world’s most significant public health challenges, with an estimated 10.7 million people falling ill and 1.23 million losing their lives to the disease in 2024 alone. 1 It is estimated that approximately a quarter of the global population may have been infected with the bacteria that cause tuberculosis. 2 While the infection can lie dormant without causing symptoms, 5 to 10% of cases will progress to symptomatic disease during a person’s lifetime. 3 Diagnosis of tuberculosis infection is essential to breaking the cycle of disease progression and transmission, preventing severe health complications, and guiding decisions about preventive treatment in high-risk populations. Expanding access to reliable diagnostics remains critical to achieving the World Health Organization’s (WHO) global elimination targets, which aim to decrease tuberculosis deaths by 90% and new cases by 80% by 2030. 4 Blood-based IGRA tests and tuberculin skin tests are established methods for identifying tuberculosis infections. In addition to offering higher accuracy and reliability, IGRAs provide a less invasive alternative to skin tests, requiring only a single patient visit and reducing dependency on specialised healthcare staff.  Unlike tuberculin skin tests, IGRAs show minimal interference from Bacillus Calmette-Guérin (BCG) vaccinations, which remain widely used worldwide. 5 Traditional IGRA testing often involves labour-intensive workflows and manual procedures that significantly burden laboratory resources. These demanding processes not only increase the risk of errors, but also create barriers for laboratories attempting to implement more reliable blood-based testing methods or scale their operations to meet the rising demand for tuberculosis infection diagnostics. By pairing Roche’s new Elecsys IGRA TB test with existing molecular cobas MTB and cobas MTB-RIF/INH assays, laboratories gain an integrated solution for managing both tuberculosis infection and active disease. The Elecsys IGRA TB assay will also feature a novel digital tool to fully automate result calculation, interpretation and reporting. This ensures rapid, reliable, and fully traceable results while freeing up valuable laboratory resources. To further reduce manual laboratory workloads, the Elecsys IGRA TB assay is designed to integrate seamlessly with third-party automated liquid handlers for front-end processes prior to tube incubation. Additionally, Roche’s long-term automation roadmap includes plans to introduce fully integrated, proprietary front-end automation. This will allow laboratories to optimize their workflows now while preparing for complete, end-to-end operational efficiency in the future. Clinical data The performance of Elecsys IGRA TB was evaluated in a global, multi-centre study conducted at both low-incidence and high-incidence settings in the European, Western Pacific, African regions and regions of the Americas. In the intended use population, i.e. individuals currently requiring tuberculosis infection testing as part of their clinical care, Elecsys IGRA TB achieved 91.12% positive percent agreement (PPA) and 94.57% negative percent agreement (NPA) when compared to standard of care methods. In bacteriologically confirmed TB disease patients, Elecsys IGRA TB demonstrated a relative sensitivity of 100% compared to an established IGRA method. Furthermore, in accordance with established clinical trial protocols, the specificity of Elecys IGRA TB was estimated in a low-risk cohort and demonstrated a specificity of 95.32 % 6 . These results together demonstrate a highly reliable performance. About Roche Roche (SIX: RO, ROP; OTCQX: RHHBY) is a healthcare company uniquely placed to prevent, stop and cure diseases by uniting leading science and technology across diagnostics, medicines and digital solutions. Roche was founded in Basel, Switzerland in 1896 and today is a leading provider of transformative medicines and diagnostics for millions of people in over 150 countries around the world. It is dedicated to tackling healthcare challenges that place the greatest strain on patients, families, communities and healthcare systems. Across its Diagnostics and Pharmaceutical divisions, Roche focuses on areas including oncology, neurology, cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, ophthalmology, infectious diseases and immunology with the aim of providing real and positive change for patients, the people they love and the professionals who care for them. Genentech in the United States is a fully owned subsidiary in the Roche Group. Roche is the majority shareholder in Chugai Pharmaceutical, a major innovator in the Japanese therapeutic antibody market. For more information, please visit www.roche.com .
